comparison_note: Specifications describe different products and form factors; they are not a universal benchmark. Workload fit, software, thermals, system configuration, precision, batch size, and model architecture can reverse a simple ranking.

metrics.label: Memory capacity
metrics.question: Does the workload fit?
metrics.explanation: VRAM or addressable unified memory is a hard gate. If model weights, activations, context/KV cache, and runtime overhead do not fit, the system must offload, quantize further, split across devices, or stop.

metrics.label: Memory bandwidth
metrics.question: How fast can data feed the cores?
metrics.explanation: A workload that fits can still wait on memory. Bus width, memory generation, clocking, cache, and access pattern determine effective bandwidth; capacity alone does not.

metrics.label: Compute engines
metrics.question: How much math can happen in parallel?
metrics.explanation: CUDA/stream processors, Tensor or matrix engines, supported numerical formats, clock speed, and architecture determine usable throughput. Core counts compare cleanly only within closely related architectures.

metrics.label: Interconnect
metrics.question: What happens beyond one chip?
metrics.explanation: PCIe, NVLink, CXL, Infinity Fabric, or an on-package coherent link determines how expensive it is to reach CPU memory, another GPU, storage, or the network.

metrics.label: Power and cooling
metrics.question: Can performance be sustained?
metrics.explanation: Board power, voltage limits, cooling, rack density, and facility power determine sustained clocks and total system cost—not just a peak specification.

metrics.label: Software path
metrics.question: Can the application use the hardware?
metrics.explanation: Drivers, compilers, kernels, framework support, quantization formats, media engines, and vendor libraries determine how much theoretical hardware becomes real performance.

gpus.name: GeForce RTX 4060 Ti 16GB
gpus.best_read: More capacity can keep a larger local model or creative project resident, even when the GPU has less compute and a narrower memory path than the 4070 SUPER.

gpus.name: GeForce RTX 4070 SUPER 12GB
gpus.best_read: For a workload that fits inside 12GB, the additional compute, wider bus, and faster memory can matter more than the 4060 Ti’s extra 4GB. It still cannot make a 14GB allocation fit locally.

gpus.name: GeForce RTX 4090 24GB
gpus.best_read: Capacity and throughput rise together, but multi-GPU scaling, ECC, virtualization, enterprise support, and data-center interconnect remain different from an accelerator platform.

gpus.name: Apple M4 Max
gpus.ai_compute: Architecture-specific; not directly comparable to NVIDIA AI TOPS
gpus.best_read: A large shared pool can fit models that exceed consumer VRAM, but shared capacity is not equivalent to 128GB of HBM and does not imply data-center accelerator throughput.
gpus.source_label: Apple M4 Pro and M4 Max announcement

gpus.name: NVIDIA H200 SXM
gpus.best_read: HBM bandwidth, ECC, NVLink, virtualization, supported systems, and enterprise software make this a cluster component—not a larger gaming card.

model_fit_examples.label: 7B-class · 4-bit
model_fit_examples.note: Weights plus a moderate runtime/context allowance.

model_fit_examples.label: 14B-class · 4-bit
model_fit_examples.note: Runtime, quantization format, context length, and KV cache decide the boundary.

model_fit_examples.label: 32B-class · 4-bit
model_fit_examples.note: Usually needs CPU/unified-memory offload, multiple GPUs, or a higher-capacity device.

memory_architectures.label: Discrete GDDR
memory_architectures.path: CPU memory ⇄ PCIe ⇄ GPU cache/cores ⇄ GDDR
memory_architectures.constraint: A separate VRAM pool makes capacity a hard local-fit gate; PCIe transfers are much slower than on-board memory.

memory_architectures.label: Unified SoC memory
memory_architectures.path: CPU + GPU + media engines ⇄ one physical memory pool
memory_architectures.strength: Large allocations can be shared without copying between separate CPU and GPU pools.
memory_architectures.constraint: Shared address space does not remove bandwidth contention or turn system memory into HBM.

memory_architectures.label: On-package HBM
memory_architectures.path: Accelerator die ⇄ very wide package interconnect ⇄ stacked HBM
memory_architectures.strength: Extremely high bandwidth and capacity close to the accelerator for training and high-throughput inference.
memory_architectures.constraint: Advanced packaging, HBM supply, thermal design, power, and cost become system gates.

memory_architectures.label: Coherent CPU–GPU
memory_architectures.path: CPU LPDDR ⇄ NVLink-C2C ⇄ GPU HBM with shared addressability
memory_architectures.strength: The GPU can address a much larger memory domain with lower overhead than conventional PCIe offload.
memory_architectures.constraint: Memory placement still matters: remote or CPU-attached memory has different bandwidth and latency from local HBM.

materials.what: Quartz-derived silicon → electronic-grade polysilicon → single-crystal ingot → polished 300 mm wafer
materials.who: Silicon producers; wafer makers including Shin-Etsu, SUMCO, GlobalWafers, Siltronic, and SK Siltron
materials.where: Raw and processed silicon is global; leading 300 mm wafer production is concentrated in Japan, Taiwan, Germany, Singapore, and South Korea
materials.when: Before every logic and memory fab step
materials.why: This is the crystalline substrate in which most GPU and DRAM transistors are formed.
materials.how: Extreme purification, crystal pulling, slicing, lapping, polishing, epitaxy, and repeated fab qualification
materials.myth: Rare earths are not the silicon substrate of a GPU.

materials.what: Copper plus small quantities of tungsten, tantalum, tin, gold, cobalt, and other specialty metals
materials.who: Miners, refiners, sputtering-target suppliers, chemical suppliers, substrate and PCB manufacturers
materials.where: Mining and refining are geographically distributed but individual high-purity forms and process steps have concentrated supplier bases
materials.when: Inside chip interconnect layers, package substrates, boards, power delivery, connectors, and data-center cabling
materials.why: A chip must move signals and power reliably; transistor density is useless without interconnect and packaging.
materials.how: Deposition, electroplating, chemical-mechanical polishing, vias, soldering, bonding, and multilayer board fabrication
materials.myth: The GPU die is only one part of the electrical system that makes an accelerator usable.

materials.what: Gallium and germanium compounds used in selected semiconductors, photonics, fiber optics, infrared systems, and power/RF devices
materials.who: Often recovered as byproducts, then refined into high-purity metals, oxides, gases, or compound-semiconductor feedstocks
materials.where: China is a leading producer/refiner; additional processing occurs in the United States, Canada, Belgium, Germany, Japan, and elsewhere
materials.when: In optical networking, sensing, power conversion, RF, and selected fab gases—not as the bulk substrate of a conventional silicon GPU
materials.why: AI systems depend on networking, optics, power, and fab inputs as well as logic dies.
materials.how: Byproduct recovery, zone refining, chemical conversion, crystal growth, epitaxy, and device fabrication
materials.myth: A mineral can be critical to the AI system without being a transistor material in the GPU core.

materials.what: Magnet rare earths such as neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium, and terbium
materials.who: Miners, separators, metal/alloy producers, and permanent-magnet manufacturers
materials.where: In 2024 China accounted for 60% of mining, 91% of refining, and 94% of sintered permanent-magnet production for magnet rare earths
materials.when: In motors, pumps, fans, robotics, power systems, storage drives, and precision fab equipment across the wider AI infrastructure
materials.why: The refining and magnet-manufacturing concentration is a system-level supply risk.
materials.how: Ore concentration → chemical separation → oxide → metal/alloy powder → sintered permanent magnet
materials.myth: Their main AI role is in machines and infrastructure around chips, not in replacing silicon wafers.

materials.what: Ultra-high-purity gases, photoresists, acids, solvents, slurries, and specialty chemicals
materials.who: Specialist chemical and industrial-gas suppliers qualified to exact fab recipes
materials.where: Production is global, but purity, transport, storage, and customer qualification make substitutions slow
materials.when: At lithography, deposition, etch, implant, clean, polish, and metrology steps—often hundreds of times per wafer
materials.why: A fab can have tools and wafers yet still stop if a qualified process input is unavailable.
materials.how: Closed-loop delivery systems meter materials at extreme purity and precision under tightly controlled recipes
materials.myth: Semiconductor supply is not reducible to mines and lithography machines.

fab_projects.name: TSMC Arizona
fab_projects.function: Leading-edge logic plus planned advanced packaging
fab_projects.production: Fab 1 entered volume production in late 2024; Fab 2 target 2028
fab_projects.elapsed: ~4 years from initial announcement to first volume production
fab_projects.lesson: A working fab is only the first layer: later phases add newer nodes, packaging, suppliers, utilities, and trained labor.

fab_projects.name: Intel Ohio One
fab_projects.production: Mod 1 operations planned for 2030–31; Mod 2 for 2032
fab_projects.elapsed: ~8–10 years from construction start to planned operations
fab_projects.lesson: The building schedule is an economic variable: Intel slowed construction while preserving the option to accelerate if customer demand warrants.

fab_projects.name: Micron Central New York
fab_projects.production: Long-horizon sequential build; precise first-output date remains demand- and execution-dependent
fab_projects.investment: Up to $100B for four fabs over 20+ years
fab_projects.elapsed: Not yet measurable—vertical construction began in 2026
fab_projects.lesson: Permits, environmental review, site utilities, skilled construction, and sequential fit-out precede process qualification and yield ramp.

fab_projects.name: SK hynix West Lafayette
fab_projects.production: Operations scheduled by the end of 2028
fab_projects.lesson: AI capacity depends on memory packaging and process integration, not only new logic-wafer fabs.

conversion_options.label: Keep the mature line
conversion_options.best_when: Demand is durable for analog, power, microcontrollers, connectivity, automotive, industrial, or long-lifecycle products.
conversion_options.benefit: Avoids write-offs and downtime; uses qualified tools, recipes, staff, and customer designs.
conversion_options.cost: Opportunity cost if another product could earn more from the same cleanroom, utilities, or wafer starts.
conversion_options.hidden_gate: Customers may value reliability, longevity, and qualification more than transistor density.

conversion_options.label: Retool within a process family
conversion_options.best_when: The shell, utilities, wafer size, contamination controls, and a meaningful share of tools can support the target process.
conversion_options.benefit: Can reuse site infrastructure and trained operations while adding targeted capacity or efficiency.
conversion_options.cost: Tool purchases, line downtime, recipe transfer, yield learning, and customer requalification can erase the apparent savings.
conversion_options.hidden_gate: A ‘node change’ may require different lithography, deposition, etch, metrology, masks, materials, and design rules.

conversion_options.label: Repurpose the facility
conversion_options.best_when: The location and cleanroom remain valuable but front-end wafer economics do not—for example, a move toward packaging, test, R&D, or specialty processes.
conversion_options.benefit: Preserves part of the physical and workforce investment while filling another supply-chain gap.
conversion_options.cost: The facility may still need major utility, vibration, floor-loading, airflow, and workflow changes.
conversion_options.hidden_gate: Packaging and test have different equipment, material flows, customers, and qualification regimes.

conversion_options.label: Build a new leading-edge fab
conversion_options.best_when: The target node, wafer flow, automation, EUV toolset, power, water, and future expansion need a purpose-built design.
conversion_options.benefit: Optimizes layout and utilities for the process and avoids forcing atomic-scale manufacturing into an unsuitable shell.
conversion_options.cost: $20–25B can be required for one leading-edge fab before ecosystem and schedule risk, according to 2026 SIA testimony.
conversion_options.hidden_gate: Capital has no value until tools, materials, people, process recipes, customers, and yield converge.

conversion_clock.label: Demand case
conversion_clock.detail: Forecast product mix, price, wafer starts, customer life cycles, and downside scenarios.

conversion_clock.label: Physical feasibility
conversion_clock.detail: Audit cleanroom class, vibration, floor loading, power, water, gases, waste treatment, wafer size, and tool compatibility.

conversion_clock.label: Tool and process transfer
conversion_clock.detail: Procure or move tools, install facilities, transfer recipes, masks, metrology, and statistical controls.

conversion_clock.label: Qualification
conversion_clock.detail: Prove reliability and let customers validate redesigned or transferred products; long-lifecycle sectors can take longer.

conversion_clock.label: Yield ramp
conversion_clock.detail: Raise the share of good dies per wafer until cost, cycle time, and delivery are commercially viable.
